domainScope: Anglemire v. Policemen's Benevolent Association of Chicago

selectedCase.title: Anglemire v. Policemen's Benevolent Association of Chicago
selectedCase.court: Appellate Court of Illinois, Second District
selectedCase.summary: Clinton H. Anglemire, a certificate holder in a fraternal benefit society, orally promised a woman that if she married him he would surrender an existing certificate and procure a new one naming her beneficiary. They married, and a certificate naming her beneficiary was issued. Later Anglemire executed an affidavit under the association's bylaws to cancel that certificate and obtain a new one naming other beneficiaries. After his death the competing claimants sued; the trial court awarded benefits to the wife based on the pre‑marriage oral promise and estoppel. The Appellate Court reversed, holding that an oral executory promise made in consideration of marriage is within the statute of frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable; subsequent marriage does not constitute sufficient part performance to remove the agreement from the statute. The insured therefore was free to change beneficiaries under the association's procedures, and the later certificate controlled.
selectedCase.relevance: This case cleanly illustrates (1) the statute of frauds rule that agreements made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) must be in writing, (2) that subsequent marriage is not part performance to avoid the statute, and (3) the limits of estoppel where the underlying promise is void under the statute. It is useful for benchmarking systems on identifying statutory‑writing requirements, exceptions (part performance/estoppel), and interactions between contractual promises and procedural/bylaw mechanisms governing beneficiary changes.

analysisDomains.name: Statute of Frauds — agreements made in consideration of marriage
analysisDomains.description: Whether the oral promise (to surrender one certificate and procure a new one naming the spouse) falls within the statute of frauds so as to require a written memorandum; includes the distinction between mutual promises to marry (not within statute) and other promises made in consideration of marriage (within statute).

analysisDomains.name: Form and timing of the promise — executory promise vs. executed performance and merger into later instruments
analysisDomains.description: Whether the promise was an executory agreement that required writing, or whether subsequent issuance of a written certificate (or other actions) effectuated, merged, or supplanted the original oral promise; examines when and how later documents can create or extinguish rights purportedly created by the oral promise.

analysisDomains.name: Part performance and equitable estoppel exceptions to the statute of frauds
analysisDomains.description: Whether the spouses’ actions (marriage itself and any acts taken in reliance) constitute sufficient part performance or give rise to equitable estoppel so as to remove the transaction from the statute of frauds and make the oral promise enforceable; assesses what kinds of acts courts treat as unequivocally referable to the alleged promise.

analysisDomains.name: Interaction with association bylaws and procedural beneficiary-change mechanisms
analysisDomains.description: How the fraternal society’s internal procedures (e.g., affidavits, surrender and issuance of certificates, formal beneficiary-change rules) affect parties’ rights; includes whether compliance with bylaw procedures produces an independently enforceable beneficiary designation and whether the association can rely on its formal process against competing equitable claims.

analysisDomains.name: Priority among competing beneficiary claims and evidentiary burdens
analysisDomains.description: Which document or act controls competing claims to the benefits (original certificate, orally promised change, subsequently issued certificate naming others), who bears the burden of proof, and what evidence is required to prove an enforceable change or to overcome a written designation.

analysisDomains.name: Available remedies and defenses when statute bars the oral promise
analysisDomains.description: What legal and equitable remedies remain (e.g., constructive trust, restitution, damages) and what defenses prevail (statute of frauds, parol evidence, compliance with bylaws) when an oral marriage‑consideration promise is unenforceable; addresses limits on estoppel and the practical relief claimants can obtain.

Anglemire v. Policemen's Benev. Ass'n of Chicago, 301 Ill.App. 277 (1939)
22 N.E.2d 713
© 2026 Thomson Reuters. No claim to original U.S. Government Works. 1
301 Ill.App. 277
Appellate Court of Illinois, Second District.
ANGLEMIRE
v.
POLICEMEN'S BENEV.
ASS'N OF CHICAGO.
POLICEMEN'S BENEV.
ASS'N OF CHICAGO
v.
ANGLEMIRE ET AL.
Gen. No. 9445.
|
Sept. 22, 1939.
Synopsis
Appeal from Circuit Court, Will County; Edwin L.
Wilson, Judge.
Action by Amanda C. Anglemire against the
Policemen's Benevolent Association of Chicago, a
corporation, to recover a sum of money as a beneficiary
under a policy of insurance, wherein the defendant by
interpleader joined Mrs. Bernice Schmelzle and others,
claimants to the fund. From a judgment for Amanda C.
Anglemire, the other parties appeal.
Reversed and remanded, with directions.
West Headnotes (3)
[1] Frauds, Statute Of Nature and
Subject of Agreement in General
185 Frauds, Statute Of
185I In General
185I(B) Agreements in Consideration of
Marriage
185k2 Nature and Subject of Agreement
in General
A parol promise by a certificate holder
of a benefit association that if plaintiff
would marry him he would name her
beneficiary in his certificate was void
under the statute of frauds requiring
promises in consideration of marriage to
be in writing, and certificate holder was
not estopped after marriage from naming
another as beneficiary. S.H.A. ch. 59, § 1.
2 Cases that cite this headnote
[2] Frauds, Statute Of Nature and
Subject of Agreement in General
185 Frauds, Statute Of
185I In General
185I(B) Agreements in Consideration of
Marriage
185k2 Nature and Subject of Agreement
in General
A verbal executory promise or agreement,
made in consideration of marriage, is
within the provision of the statute of
frauds stating that no action shall be
brought “to charge any person upon any
agreement made upon consideration of
marriage” unless the agreement on which
action is brought is in writing and signed
by party to be charged therewith. S.H.A.
ch. 59, § 1.
[3] Frauds, Statute Of Part
Performance in General
185 Frauds, Statute Of
185IX Operation and Effect of Statute
185k129 Part Performance in General
185k129(1) In General
Where an agreement made in
consideration of marriage is void under
the statute of frauds because not in
writing, subsequent marriage of parties
does not constitute a part performance so
as to take contract out of operation of the
statute. S.H.A. ch. 59, § 1.

Carvalho, Ana 3/20/2026
For Educational Use Only
Anglemire v. Policemen's Benev. Ass'n of Chicago, 301 Ill.App. 277 (1939)
22 N.E.2d 713
© 2026 Thomson Reuters. No claim to original U.S. Government Works. 2
1 Case that cites this headnote
Attorneys and Law Firms
*278 **713 England, O'Toole & Kays, of Chicago,
and Savage & Holbrook, of Joliet, for appellants.
Irving Shutts, of Joliet, for appellee.
Opinion
HUFFMAN, Justice.
Clinton H. Anglemire had for many years been
a certificate holder in the Policemen's Benevolent
Association of Chicago, a fraternal benefit society. On
November 2, 1935, he married Amanda C. Anglemire.
Following this marriage and on December 13, 1935,
pursuant to the by-laws of the Association with regard
to change of beneficiary, he had a new certificate
issued making her beneficiary therein. On November
20, 1936, pursuant to application to the Association by
the insured in accordance with the constitution and by-
laws thereof, he caused cancellation of the certificate
of December 13, 1935, and procured a new certificate
making appellants herein the beneficiaries. He died
on July 2, 1938. Following his death, Amanda C.
Anglemire, beneficiary in the former certificate, and
appellants as beneficiaries in the latter certificate, each
made claim for the benefits.
Suit was first instituted by Amanda C. Anglemire
in which she claimed that the certificate issued
on December 13, 1935, wherein she was made
beneficiary, was in full force and effect at the time
of the insured's death. She based her claim upon
a parol promise of the insured made to her prior
to the marriage, that if she would marry him, he
would surrender the certificate which he then held and
procure a new one naming her as beneficiary, and that
she relying on such promise, married the insured. She
set up the issuance of the certificate of December 13,
1935, wherein she was made beneficiary, alleged such
certificate to be in her possession, and that she was
ready and willing to surrender it upon payment of
the benefits therein provided. She *279 alleged that
she fulfilled her **714 marriage obligations to the
insured, and that he was estopped from changing the
beneficiary from her to any other person.
The Association answered this complaint alleging that
the insured prior to his death and on November 20,
1936, secured a new certificate pursuant to the by-laws
of the Association, upon his affidavit that the certificate
then existing was in the possession of his wife and
could not be produced by him; and that pursuant to
the constitution and by-laws of the Association, the
certificate of November 20, 1936, was issued in place
of that previously issued under date of December 13,
1935. The certificate of November 20, 1936, named as
beneficiaries the children and a sister of the insured.
In view of the fact that the Association had two
sets of claimants for the benefits provided in the
certificate, it filed its complaint in the nature of a bill
of interpleader making all the claimants defendants.
The cause was then transferred to the chancery docket.
The defendants made answer and hearing was had
upon their respective rights to the benefits. Appellee
based her right thereto upon the parol promise of the
insured that if she would marry him he would make her
beneficiary, and claimed that in consideration of such
promise, she married him; that following the marriage
he surrendered the old certificate and caused to issue a
new one under date of December 13, 1935, naming her
as beneficiary; and that he was estopped from changing
the beneficiary as was done by the certificate issued on
November 20, 1936. Appellants set up that her claim
was barred by the Statute of Frauds, and that her
alleged agreement made with the insured was void.
The trial court held in favor of appellee wife, and
decreed that she was entitled to the benefits accruing
under the certificate. The court found by the decree
that prior to the marriage of the insured with appellee,
he held a benefit certificate in the Association; that
prior to such marriage he promised and agreed with
*280 her that if she would marry him he would
surrender that certificate and procure a new one in
which he would name her as beneficiary; that she,
relying on such promise, married the insured; that after
the marriage he did procure a new certificate naming

-- 2 of 4 --

Carvalho, Ana 3/20/2026
For Educational Use Only
Anglemire v. Policemen's Benev. Ass'n of Chicago, 301 Ill.App. 277 (1939)
22 N.E.2d 713
© 2026 Thomson Reuters. No claim to original U.S. Government Works. 3
her as beneficiary, and of which certificate she is in
possession; that the above agreement between appellee
and the insured upon the subsequent marriage and
change of beneficiary became an executed contract;
that he was estopped from later changing same; that
his action in procuring the certificate of November 20,
1936, was void, and the beneficiaries named therein
not entitled to any of the benefits accruing under the
certificate of the insured in said Association. From the
above decree, the beneficiaries named in the certificate
of November 20, 1936, have prosecuted this appeal.
It appears under Article IV of the constitution and by-
laws of the Association that, “In case a member is
unable for any reason to surrender his certificate for
change of beneficiary, he may file with the Recording
Secretary of the Association, his affidavit, setting
forth the reasons why he is unable to surrender
his certificate, for cancellation and for the issuing
of a new certificate, and in and by such affidavit,
he may designate a new beneficiary of his choice
and such change of beneficiary by affidavit shall
be binding upon the member, his beneficiary and
the Association.” It appears by the testimony of Mr.
Christensen, a member of the Chicago police force
and a Trustee of the Association, that it was his duty
to collect dues from the members and to take care
of the matter of change of beneficiaries. He states
that he knew the insured and that he took care of the
change of beneficiary when the same was changed to
appellee in December, 1935, and also took care of the
last change in beneficiary from her to appellants. The
insured came in person to see this witness regarding the
last change in beneficiary. He stated that he was unable
to produce the certificate then outstanding because
*281 his wife had it, whereupon he was permitted to
fill out an affidavit for the issuance of a new certificate
pursuant to the above provision of the constitution and
by-laws. The affidavit made by the insured was upon
the regular form furnished and used by the Association.
Pursuant to the execution of the affidavit, and in
accordance with the constitution and by-laws of the
Association, the policy of November 20, 1936, making
appellants beneficiaries, was issued and delivered to
the insured, and the certificate issued on December 13,
1935, cancelled.
It appears that the marriage was not a happy one.
The insured was of advanced years and considerably
older than appellee. Their difficulties at times assumed
a violent nature. In the early part of November, **715
1936, appellee took possession of certain securities
of the insured, including the policy in which she was
beneficiary. This precipitated a personal encounter
between them, wherein the insured was not the
winner. Appellee retained the policy. Soon thereafter
the insured made the change of beneficiary by the
cancellation of the certificate of December 13, 1935,
and procuring issuance of that of November 20, 1936.
[1] [2] The English statute of frauds provides that
no action should be brought to charge any person on
any agreement made in consideration of marriage,
unless the agreement or some memorandum or note
thereof should be in writing and signed by the party to
be charged therewith or some person thereunto by him
lawfully authorized. In this country, this provision has
been generally adopted by the various jurisdictions.
And contrary to the rule which prevails in cases of
other contracts, the subsequent marriage of the parties
is not considered to constitute such a part performance
as to take the contract out of the operation of that
provision of the statute of frauds directed against
contracts in consideration of marriage. The rule
in such case is based upon the express language of
the *282 statute. Promise of marriage, followed
by the marriage, is the exact situation contemplated
by the statute. The marriage adds nothing to the
circumstances set out by the statutory provision which
makes a writing essential. The promise in itself being
a nullity, produces no obligation, and any subsequent
act of the husband following the marriage must be
considered as purely voluntary. Thus the agreement
being void under the statute of frauds, the act of
the husband after marriage even though in view of
such agreement, must be deemed to be without legal
consideration to support it, and as above said, stands
therefore upon the same basis as if such act were
purely voluntary. Hence the insured had the right to
change the beneficiary in the policy as provided by the
constitution and by-laws of the Association.
[3] Under the English statute of frauds, and similar
enactments in the various jurisdictions in this country,

-- 3 of 4 --

Carvalho, Ana 3/20/2026
For Educational Use Only
Anglemire v. Policemen's Benev. Ass'n of Chicago, 301 Ill.App. 277 (1939)
22 N.E.2d 713
© 2026 Thomson Reuters. No claim to original U.S. Government Works. 4
promises in consideration of marriage, other than
the mutual promise to marry, are required to be in
writing; and an oral promise to settle property upon an
intended spouse is void. It is well settled in this state
that a verbal executory promise or agreement, made
in consideration of marriage, is embraced within
the provision of the statute of frauds, requiring that
actions, “to charge any person upon any agreement
made upon consideration of marriage, * * * shall
be in writing, and signed by the party to be charged
therewith.” McAnnulty v. McAnnulty, 120 Ill. 26,
11 N.E. 397, 60 Am.Rep. 552; Richardson v.
Richardson, 148 Ill. 563, 36 N.E. 608, 26 L.R.A. 305;
Keady v. White, 168 Ill. 76, 48 N.E. 314; Austin v.
Kuehn, 211 Ill. 113, 71 N.E. 841; Garlock v. Campbell,
263 Ill.App. 287.
Appellee bases her right to the benefits of the
certificate upon an oral promise of the insured, made
in consideration of marriage. Under sec. 1 of ch. 59
of the statutes, Ill.Rev.Stat.1937 (commonly called the
statute of frauds), such an agreement was void, and
the subsequent marriage *283 did not take the same
out of the operation of the statute.
The decree is reversed and the cause remanded to the
trial court with directions to enter judgment in favor of
appellants.
Reversed and remanded with directions.
All Citations
301 Ill.App. 277, 22 N.E.2d 713
End of Document © 2026 Thomson Reuters. No claim to original U.S.
Government Works.

sourceIntake.sourceQualityRating: High. The grounding pattern is tightly doctrinal, fact-specific, and well-suited to testing statute-of-frauds analysis, formal beneficiary-change rules, and limits on equity.
sourceIntake.recommendation: Use as a model for writing answers that distinguish oral marriage-consideration promises from written beneficiary changes, and that treat marriage alone as insufficient part performance absent stronger equitable facts.
jdReviewBurden: Check whether the alleged promise is truly in consideration of marriage or instead a separate enforceable undertaking.
Confirm whether a valid later writing or designation exists under the controlling instrument.
Assess whether reliance facts are unusually strong enough to support estoppel or constructive trust.
Verify whether the governing jurisdiction recognizes substantial-compliance or equitable exceptions in beneficiary-change cases.

sourceExtraction.legalIssue: Whether an oral promise to change beneficiary or property rights in exchange for marriage is enforceable despite the statute of frauds, and whether later marriage, reliance, estoppel, or a later formal designation changes the result.
sourceExtraction.blackLetterRule: Agreements made in consideration of marriage generally must be in writing to be enforceable; an executory oral promise of that kind is within the statute of frauds. Marriage alone is ordinarily not sufficient part performance to avoid the statute, and equitable estoppel is limited when it would defeat a clear statutory writing requirement. A later valid written beneficiary designation made in compliance with governing rules typically controls.
triggerFacts: An oral promise to provide or redirect benefits if the promisee marries the promisor.
Subsequent marriage allegedly performed in reliance on that promise.
Later execution of a formal affidavit, designation, or other document changing the beneficiary under association rules.
Competing claims between an earlier promised beneficiary and a later formally designated beneficiary.
sourceExtraction.holding: The likely outcome pattern is that the oral marriage-based promise is unenforceable, marriage does not remove it from the statute of frauds, and the later compliant written designation governs the benefits.
limits: The rule is narrower when the promise is memorialized in writing or when the later instrument is defective, unauthorized, or procured by fraud.
Separate equitable claims may remain possible if there is independent misconduct beyond mere nonperformance of the oral promise.
Outcomes can vary if the governing bylaws or policy language permit substantial compliance or create a different change-of-beneficiary framework.
uncertainty: How broadly a court will apply estoppel when reliance is substantial but the promise is statutorily void.
Whether a particular later instrument should be treated as a new contract, a mere administrative act, or a valid superseding designation.
The extent to which local law allows equitable relief in the face of strict statutory formalities.

results.domainName: Statute of Frauds — agreements made in consideration of marriage
results.label: Direct fit
results.explanation: This is the core doctrine of the case: the court held that the oral promise made in consideration of marriage fell within the statute of frauds and therefore had to be in writing to be enforceable.

results.domainName: Form and timing of the promise — executory promise vs. executed performance and merger into later instruments
results.label: Direct fit
results.explanation: The case turns on whether the oral promise remained an unenforceable executory agreement and whether later actions or the later certificate displaced it; the court held the subsequent certificate controlled rather than the prior oral promise.

results.domainName: Part performance and equitable estoppel exceptions to the statute of frauds
results.label: Direct fit
results.explanation: The court expressly addressed and rejected the argument that marriage itself was sufficient part performance and limited the use of estoppel against an agreement barred by the statute of frauds.

results.domainName: Interaction with association bylaws and procedural beneficiary-change mechanisms
results.label: Direct fit
results.explanation: The beneficiary dispute depended on the fraternal association’s bylaws and formal change procedures, which the court treated as controlling once the insured complied with them.

results.domainName: Priority among competing beneficiary claims and evidentiary burdens
results.label: Direct fit
results.explanation: The case resolves competing claims between the wife and the later-named beneficiaries and necessarily involves which designation controlled and what proof was sufficient to establish an enforceable right to the proceeds.

results.domainName: Available remedies and defenses when statute bars the oral promise
results.label: Direct fit
results.explanation: The opinion addresses the defenses that defeat enforcement of the oral promise, especially the statute of frauds and limits on estoppel, and thereby determines what relief is unavailable to the claimant.

benchmarkAnswer:
Bottom line: In similar disputes, an oral executory promise made in exchange for marriage is generally unenforceable unless it is memorialized in a signed writing, and the later marriage itself usually does not supply the kind of part performance needed to remove the agreement from the statute of frauds. Where the controlling instrument or governing association rules require formal steps to change a beneficiary, a later compliant written designation will ordinarily control over an earlier oral assurance. Estoppel and equitable remedies may still be argued, but they are usually limited when the underlying promise is the very kind of agreement the statute requires to be in writing.

### Statute of Frauds — agreements made in consideration of marriage
**Governing rule:** Agreements made in consideration of marriage, other than mutual promises to marry, commonly fall within the statute of frauds and must be supported by a writing signed by the party to be charged. An oral promise to transfer, surrender, revoke, or redesignate benefits in exchange for marrying is the type of promise that is typically unenforceable if it remains executory.

**Application pattern:** If the alleged bargain is “marry me and I will change the beneficiary,” the most likely outcome is that the promise is treated as a marriage-consideration agreement subject to the statute. The strongest counterargument is that the promise may be characterized as an independent post-marriage undertaking or as a completed transfer rather than a purely executory contract, but that usually depends on clear written evidence or unequivocal execution. Without such proof, the oral promise is unlikely to be enforced.

### Form and timing of the promise — executory promise vs. executed performance and merger into later instruments
**Governing rule:** A promise that is still executory when enforcement is sought generally must satisfy the statute of frauds; later documents may create new rights, supersede prior understandings, or evidence a separate enforceable transaction. If a later instrument is properly executed under the controlling rules, it can replace earlier informal arrangements.

**Application pattern:** Where a later written certificate, designation form, affidavit, or comparable instrument is issued or executed in compliance with the relevant formalities, courts usually treat that writing as the operative source of rights. The earlier oral assurance may be relevant as background, but it ordinarily does not override the later formal instrument. The result can change if the later writing is defective, was procured by fraud, or was never validly delivered or accepted.

### Part performance and equitable estoppel exceptions to the statute of frauds
**Governing rule:** Part performance and equitable estoppel may sometimes prevent a party from invoking the statute of frauds, but the acts relied on must usually be clearly and unequivocally referable to the alleged agreement. Mere performance of a separate legal duty, or conduct that is equally consistent with another explanation, is usually insufficient.

**Application pattern:** Marriage itself is commonly not enough, by itself, to take a marriage-consideration promise out of the statute. Additional reliance acts may help only if they point strongly and uniquely to the promised exchange and if refusing enforcement would create a true equitable injustice. The strongest counterargument is that one spouse changed position in reliance on the promise and that the promisor should be estopped from benefitting from the marriage after inducing it. Even so, estoppel is often narrowly applied when it would effectively nullify the statute’s writing requirement.

### Interaction with association bylaws and procedural beneficiary-change mechanisms
**Governing rule:** When benefits are governed by an association’s bylaws, certificate terms, or policy procedures, beneficiary changes generally must comply with those internal requirements to be effective. Courts often give controlling weight to the formal mechanism specified by the governing instrument.

**Application pattern:** If the member later follows the prescribed affidavit, surrender, or reissuance process, that compliance tends to produce an independently effective beneficiary change, even if there was an earlier informal promise to the contrary. A competing claimant must then show that the formal change was invalid, unauthorized, or ineffective under the governing rules. The main uncertainty is whether the bylaws leave room for substantial compliance or equitable override; that depends on the wording of the instrument and the jurisdiction’s approach to beneficiary-change formalities.

### Priority among competing beneficiary claims and evidentiary burdens
**Governing rule:** The claimant relying on an oral promise generally bears the burden of proving an enforceable right that defeats the written designation or formal beneficiary change. Written designations and compliance with governing procedures are typically strong evidence of priority.

**Application pattern:** When there is a later valid written beneficiary designation, the default rule is that it controls unless the challenger can show invalidity, fraud, lack of capacity, undue influence, failure to comply with required formalities, or another recognized basis for setting it aside. The strongest counterargument is that the later designation should be disregarded because the decedent was already contractually bound to benefit someone else. But that argument usually fails if the supposed prior obligation is oral, unenforceable, or not sufficiently proven.

### Available remedies and defenses when statute bars the oral promise
**Governing rule:** If the oral marriage-based promise is barred by the statute of frauds, direct contract enforcement is usually unavailable. Depending on the facts, a claimant may still seek limited equitable relief such as restitution, constructive trust, or another remedy tied to identifiable wrongdoing, but those remedies are not automatic and often require an independent equitable basis.

**Application pattern:** The usual defenses are the statute of frauds, the parol-evidence rule if a final writing exists, and strict compliance with the beneficiary-change process. Relief is most likely only when there is separate fraud, unjust enrichment, or a defect in the later formal designation. The uncertain area is how far a court will go in using equity to soften the statute when the claimant has relied on the promise and the promisor later uses formal procedures to defeat that expectation. In the ordinary case, however, equity does not rescue an otherwise void oral promise.
benchmarkQuestion:
Title: Marriage Promise and Benefit Designation

Facts:
A member of a fraternal benefit Party D held a death-benefit certificate that named his adult daughter as the beneficiary. Before he married, he told his fiancée that if she agreed to marry him, he would cancel the existing certificate and obtain a new one naming her as the sole beneficiary. She agreed, and they married a few months later. After the wedding, the Party D issued a new certificate naming the wife as beneficiary. The Party D’s governing rules, however, allowed a member to change beneficiaries at any time by signing a prescribed affidavit, surrendering the current certificate, and obtaining a replacement certificate. Several years later, after a falling out with his wife, the member completed the required affidavit, surrendered the wife-beneficiary certificate, and secured a new certificate naming his sister and nephew as beneficiaries. He died shortly afterward without leaving any will dealing with the certificate proceeds. The wife and the later-named beneficiaries both claim the death benefits. The wife argues that the pre-marriage promise created an enforceable contractual right to the proceeds, that her marriage was enough reliance to bind the member, and that the later change should be disregarded because it violated the promise. The later-named beneficiaries argue that the promise was never enforceable because it was not in writing, that the member followed the Party D’s procedures for changing beneficiaries, and that the later certificate controls.

Tasks:
1. Statute of Frauds — agreements made in consideration of marriage: Does the member’s oral promise to change the beneficiary in exchange for the fiancée’s agreement to marry fall within the statute of frauds, and if so, what is the significance of the lack of a signed writing?
2. Form and timing of the promise — executory promise vs. executed performance and merger into later instruments: Should the later issuance of a certificate naming the wife be treated as independently enforceable performance, as a merger of the oral promise into a written instrument, or as something that leaves the original oral promise still controlling?
3. Part performance and equitable estoppel exceptions to the statute of frauds: Do the marriage itself, the wife’s reliance, or any other acts here amount to part performance or create an estoppel that would prevent the member or the later beneficiaries from invoking the statute of frauds?
4. Interaction with Party D bylaws and procedural beneficiary-change mechanisms: How do the Party D’s affidavit-and-surrender procedures affect the effectiveness of the later beneficiary change, and do those procedures create rights that override the prior oral understanding?
5. Priority among competing beneficiary claims and evidentiary burdens: Which beneficiary designation should control on these facts, and which party bears the burden of proving a valid right to the death benefits or a legally ineffective change?
6. Available remedies and defenses when statute bars the oral promise: If the wife cannot enforce the oral promise directly, what remedies, if any, might still be available to her, and what defenses are most likely to defeat them?

Answer Format:
Write a structured legal memo organized by the numbered tasks above and end with a clear bottom-line conclusion.

savedPrompts.prompt:
Legal domain: Contracts
Anchor case title: Anglemire v. Policemen's Benevolent Association of Chicago
Citation: 301 Ill. App. 277, 22 N.E.2d 713 (Ill. App. 1939)
Court: Appellate Court of Illinois, Second District
Year: 1939
Source URL: Anglemire_v_Policemens_Benev_Assn_of_Chicago.pdf
Case summary: Clinton H. Anglemire, a certificate holder in a fraternal benefit society, orally promised a woman that if she married him he would surrender an existing certificate and procure a new one naming her beneficiary. They married, and a certificate naming her beneficiary was issued. Later Anglemire executed an affidavit under the association's bylaws to cancel that certificate and obtain a new one naming other beneficiaries. After his death the competing claimants sued; the trial court awarded benefits to the wife based on the pre‑marriage oral promise and estoppel. The Appellate Court reversed, holding that an oral executory promise made in consideration of marriage is within the statute of frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable; subsequent marriage does not constitute sufficient part performance to remove the agreement from the statute. The insured therefore was free to change beneficiaries under the association's procedures, and the later certificate controlled.
Why selected: This case cleanly illustrates (1) the statute of frauds rule that agreements made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) must be in writing, (2) that subsequent marriage is not part performance to avoid the statute, and (3) the limits of estoppel where the underlying promise is void under the statute. It is useful for benchmarking systems on identifying statutory‑writing requirements, exceptions (part performance/estoppel), and interactions between contractual promises and procedural/bylaw mechanisms governing beneficiary changes.

Analysis domains:
1. Statute of Frauds — agreements made in consideration of marriage: Whether the oral promise (to surrender one certificate and procure a new one naming the spouse) falls within the statute of frauds so as to require a written memorandum; includes the distinction between mutual promises to marry (not within statute) and other promises made in consideration of marriage (within statute).
2. Form and timing of the promise — executory promise vs. executed performance and merger into later instruments: Whether the promise was an executory agreement that required writing, or whether subsequent issuance of a written certificate (or other actions) effectuated, merged, or supplanted the original oral promise; examines when and how later documents can create or extinguish rights purportedly created by the oral promise.
3. Part performance and equitable estoppel exceptions to the statute of frauds: Whether the spouses’ actions (marriage itself and any acts taken in reliance) constitute sufficient part performance or give rise to equitable estoppel so as to remove the transaction from the statute of frauds and make the oral promise enforceable; assesses what kinds of acts courts treat as unequivocally referable to the alleged promise.
4. Interaction with association bylaws and procedural beneficiary-change mechanisms: How the fraternal society’s internal procedures (e.g., affidavits, surrender and issuance of certificates, formal beneficiary-change rules) affect parties’ rights; includes whether compliance with bylaw procedures produces an independently enforceable beneficiary designation and whether the association can rely on its formal process against competing equitable claims.
5. Priority among competing beneficiary claims and evidentiary burdens: Which document or act controls competing claims to the benefits (original certificate, orally promised change, subsequently issued certificate naming others), who bears the burden of proof, and what evidence is required to prove an enforceable change or to overcome a written designation.
6. Available remedies and defenses when statute bars the oral promise: What legal and equitable remedies remain (e.g., constructive trust, restitution, damages) and what defenses prevail (statute of frauds, parol evidence, compliance with bylaws) when an oral marriage‑consideration promise is unenforceable; addresses limits on estoppel and the practical relief claimants can obtain.

Metadata requirements:

failureModeSeeds: Treating any oral promise connected to marriage as enforceable without addressing the statute of frauds.
Assuming that the marriage itself automatically counts as sufficient part performance to remove the agreement from the statute.
Ignoring the controlling beneficiary-change procedures in the governing bylaws or certificate terms.
Confusing an unenforceable oral promise with a later valid written designation that independently changes rights.
Overstating estoppel by using it to erase a statutory writing requirement without a strong, uniquely referable reliance showing.
masterIssueStatement: When a person makes an oral promise in consideration of marriage to change beneficiary or property-rights arrangements, does the statute of frauds bar enforcement absent a writing, and do later marriage, reliance, estoppel, or a later written designation alter that result?
